sets.passage: Many organisms that live in the deep ocean produce their own light through a chemical process called bioluminescence. Inside special cells, a molecule known as luciferin reacts with oxygen, releasing energy as a cool glow rather than as heat. Unlike the light from a bulb, almost none of this energy is wasted. Different species use the glow for different purposes. Some fish dangle a glowing lure to draw prey toward their jaws. Others flash brightly to startle a predator and escape into the darkness. Certain squid release a cloud of luminous fluid that confuses an attacker. Because sunlight cannot reach these depths, the ability to make light offers a clear advantage, shaping how animals hunt, hide, and communicate. Scientists studying these reactions have even borrowed luciferin for medical research, where the glow helps them track the activity of cells inside living tissue.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How deep-sea animals find food in groups
How and why deep-sea organisms produce light
Why the deep ocean is colder than the surface
How scientists build artificial lights

questions.prompt: According to the passage, luciferin produces light by reacting with
options: heat
salt water
oxygen
sunlight

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the ability to make light is especially valuable in the deep ocean because
options: sunlight does not reach those depths
the water there is unusually warm
few other animals live there
the pressure destroys other senses

questions.prompt: The luminous fluid released by some squid is used to
options: attract a mate
confuse an attacker
warm the surrounding water
light the way to food

sets.explain_zh: 生物学说明文。主旨/细节/推断(深海无阳光)/细节，含 1 道推断题。

sets.passage: Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, a slow and costly process that kept them rare. A single volume might take a scribe months to finish, so only wealthy institutions could build libraries. This changed when Johannes Gutenberg introduced a printing press that used movable metal type. Individual letters could be arranged into a page, inked, and pressed onto paper, then rearranged to print something else. Suddenly a workshop could produce hundreds of identical copies in the time a scribe once needed for one. As printed books became cheaper, more people learned to read, and new ideas spread across the continent far faster than before. Scholars in distant cities could now read the same text and compare their findings. Many historians argue that this flood of affordable books helped prepare the way for later scientific and religious movements, because reformers could reach a wide audience without the approval of a few powerful copyists.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Gutenberg was the first person in Europe to read widely
The printing press changed how books and ideas spread
Scribes were poorly paid for their work
Religious leaders opposed all printed books

questions.prompt: Before the printing press, books were rare mainly because
options: paper had not yet been invented
few people wanted to read them
they had to be copied slowly by hand
governments limited their sale

questions.prompt: The author mentions that a single volume could take a scribe months to finish in order to
options: praise the skill of medieval scribes
emphasize how slow copying was before the press
explain why books were often inaccurate
show that scribes enjoyed their work

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the printing press helped reformers because it allowed them to
options: reach many readers without depending on a few copyists
travel more easily between cities
write in several languages at once
avoid paying taxes on books

sets.explain_zh: 历史说明文。主旨/细节/组织(为何提及抄写耗时)/推断，含组织+推断高阶题。

sets.passage: Cities are often several degrees warmer than the countryside around them, a difference scientists call the urban heat island effect. The main cause is the materials from which cities are built. Concrete, brick, and asphalt absorb sunlight during the day and release that heat slowly at night, so urban areas cool down far less after sunset than open fields do. Tall buildings make the problem worse by blocking breezes and trapping warm air between them. Human activity adds still more heat, as cars, factories, and air conditioners all give off energy. The effect can raise health risks in summer, since nighttime temperatures stay high enough to keep residents from recovering from the day's heat. Planners have found several ways to reduce it. Planting trees provides shade and cools the air as water evaporates from their leaves. Painting roofs white reflects sunlight instead of absorbing it. Even small parks can lower temperatures in the surrounding blocks, showing that careful design can soften a city's climate.

questions.prompt: What does the passage mainly discuss?
options: Why cities grow larger than towns
The causes of the urban heat island effect and ways to reduce it
How air conditioners work
Why summers are getting hotter worldwide

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the main cause of the urban heat island effect is
options: the large number of people in cities
the materials used to build cities
a lack of rainfall in summer
pollution from distant factories

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that painting roofs white helps because a white surface
options: absorbs more heat from the air
reflects sunlight rather than absorbing it
blocks wind between buildings
produces shade for the street

questions.prompt: According to the passage, trees cool a city in part by
options: releasing water vapor as it evaporates from their leaves
absorbing heat into their trunks at night
slowing down passing cars
reflecting sunlight back into space

sets.explain_zh: 环境科学说明文。主旨/细节(成因)/推断(白屋顶)/细节(树木降温机制)。

sets.passage: For much of the twentieth century, many educators believed that learning two languages in childhood would confuse a child and slow mental development. Recent research suggests the opposite. Children who grow up speaking two languages must constantly choose which one to use and hold back the other. This mental effort, repeated countless times each day, appears to strengthen a set of skills that psychologists call executive control — the ability to focus attention, switch between tasks, and ignore distractions. Studies comparing bilingual and monolingual speakers have found that bilingual people often do better on tasks requiring such control, even when the tasks have nothing to do with language. Some research also suggests that lifelong bilingualism may delay the onset of memory disorders in old age, though scientists caution that the evidence is still incomplete. What once looked like a burden, then, may actually be a kind of mental exercise, quietly training the brain through the ordinary act of moving between two ways of speaking.

questions.prompt: What is the main point of the passage?
options: Bilingual children always earn higher grades
Speaking two languages may benefit the brain rather than harm it
Most educators still oppose early bilingualism
Memory disorders are caused by speaking one language

questions.prompt: In the passage, executive control refers to the ability to
options: speak two languages without an accent
focus attention, switch tasks, and ignore distractions
memorize long lists of words
translate quickly between languages

questions.prompt: That bilingual people do better even on tasks unrelated to language suggests that
options: the benefit applies only to language skills
the advantage is general and not limited to language
the tasks were too easy for everyone
monolingual speakers did not try hard

questions.prompt: The author begins by describing an older belief mainly in order to
options: prove that educators are usually wrong
contrast it with the newer research that follows
explain how children learn to read
warn parents against teaching two languages

sets.explain_zh: 心理/语言学说明文。主旨/词义/推断(益处普遍)/组织(开篇旧观点的作用)。

sets.passage: A coral reef may look like a colorful rock, but it is built by tiny living animals called polyps. Each polyp builds a hard cup of limestone around itself, and over centuries these skeletons pile up into the vast structures we call reefs. The polyps could not survive on their own in the clear, nutrient-poor waters where reefs grow. Instead, they depend on a partnership with single-celled algae that live inside their tissues. The algae capture sunlight and turn it into sugars, sharing this food with their hosts; in return, the polyps give the algae a safe home and the carbon dioxide they need. This close relationship explains why reefs form only in shallow, sunlit water. It also explains why reefs are fragile. When the ocean grows too warm, the polyps push out their algae, losing both their color and their main food supply. If the warmth continues, the starving coral may die, a process known as bleaching.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How fish hide among coral reefs
How a partnership between polyps and algae builds and endangers reefs
Why limestone forms in shallow water
How ocean currents move nutrients

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the algae provide the polyps with
options: a hard outer skeleton
sugars made from sunlight
protection from predators
carbon dioxide

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that reefs form only in shallow water because
options: the algae need sunlight to make food
polyps cannot survive cold temperatures
deep water lacks limestone
fish prefer to live near the surface

questions.prompt: According to the passage, bleaching begins when
options: polyps grow too quickly
the water becomes too warm and polyps expel their algae
too many fish feed on the coral
sunlight becomes too strong

sets.explain_zh: 海洋生物说明文。主旨/细节(藻类供糖)/推断(浅水需阳光)/细节(白化起因)。

sets.passage: Imagine a pasture open to all the herders of a village. Each herder gains directly by adding one more animal to graze there, since that animal belongs to them alone. The cost of the extra grazing, however, is shared by everyone, because the grass is a common resource. From the viewpoint of a single herder, adding animals always seems worthwhile. Yet if every herder reasons the same way, the pasture is soon stripped bare, and all of them lose. Economists call this situation the tragedy of the commons. It appears wherever a resource is shared but the benefits of using it are private. Ocean fisheries, clean air, and public water supplies can all be overused in this way. The pattern is not caused by greed alone; even reasonable people, acting in their own interest, can together ruin what they share. Solutions usually require some agreed limit — a rule, a fee, or shared ownership — that ties the cost of using the resource back to the user.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Herders should never share land
Shared resources can be overused when benefits are private but costs are shared
Greed is the only cause of environmental damage
Fisheries are more valuable than pastures

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does each herder keep adding animals?
options: The village orders them to
The gain is private while the cost is shared
Grass grows back quickly
Animals are inexpensive to buy

questions.prompt: The author states that the problem is not caused by greed alone in order to make the point that
options: herders are unusually selfish people
even reasonable self-interest can ruin a shared resource
greed never plays any role
the pasture was too small to begin with

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the solutions mentioned work by
options: making the user bear the cost of using the resource
removing all animals from the pasture
encouraging herders to compete harder
dividing the village into separate groups

sets.explain_zh: 经济学说明文。主旨/细节(私利公本)/组织(强调非仅贪婪)/推断(解决机制)。

sets.passage: For centuries, European painters showed distant objects simply by placing them higher on the panel, with little sense of real depth. During the early fifteenth century, artists in Italy developed a technique that changed Western painting: linear perspective. They noticed that parallel lines, such as the edges of a road, appear to draw together as they move away, finally meeting at a single point on the horizon. By drawing all such lines toward this vanishing point, a painter could make a flat surface seem to open into deep space. Figures and buildings could be placed convincingly near or far, their sizes shrinking according to fixed mathematical rules. The effect was so persuasive that viewers felt they could walk into the scene. Perspective also reflected a wider change in thinking, for it treated the painting as a window seen from one fixed human viewpoint. In this way a mathematical method and a new confidence in human observation advanced together, reshaping how Europeans pictured the world.

questions.prompt: What does the passage mainly describe?
options: The life of a famous Italian painter
The development of linear perspective and its meaning
Why Italian roads were unusually straight
How paint was made during the Renaissance

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the vanishing point is where
options: the brightest light appears
parallel lines seem to meet on the horizon
the painter signs the work
two paintings are joined together

questions.prompt: The author mentions earlier painters placing objects higher on the panel in order to
options: praise their use of color
show what perspective changed
explain why their paint faded
describe a religious tradition

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that perspective reflected a new way of thinking because it
options: treated the painting as a view from one fixed human position
required many painters to work together
used only religious subjects
ignored the rules of mathematics

sets.explain_zh: 艺术史说明文。主旨/细节(灭点)/组织(提早期画法的用意)/推断(透视反映新思维)。

sets.passage: When we learn something new, the memory is at first fragile and easily lost. Sleep appears to play a crucial role in making such memories last. During the day, new information is stored for a short time in a region of the brain called the hippocampus. While we sleep, especially during deep stages, the brain replays the day's experiences, gradually moving important memories to other areas where they can be kept for the long term. This process is known as consolidation. Experiments support the idea. People who sleep after studying a list of words or a new skill usually remember it better than those who stay awake for the same period, even when both groups are tested at the same time of day. Sleep seems to do more than simply prevent new distractions; it actively strengthens what was learned. Researchers therefore warn that students who lose sleep before an exam may harm the very memories they stayed up to build.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why people dream during deep sleep
The role of sleep in strengthening new memories
How to study for an exam quickly
Why the hippocampus is the largest part of the brain

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the hippocampus is where new information is
options: stored permanently
stored for a short time
erased during sleep
turned into a skill

questions.prompt: The experiment comparing sleepers with those who stayed awake suggests that sleep
options: only helps people avoid distractions
actively strengthens what was learned
has no effect on memory
works only for physical skills

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that a student who stays up all night before an exam may
options: remember the material better than usual
weaken the memories formed while studying
fall asleep during the test
learn a completely new subject

sets.explain_zh: 神经科学说明文。主旨/细节(海马体暂存)/推断(睡眠主动加固)/推断(熬夜损记忆)。

sets.passage: The Earth's outer shell is not a single solid layer but is broken into about a dozen large pieces called tectonic plates. These plates float on hotter, partly molten rock beneath them and move a few centimeters each year, roughly the speed at which fingernails grow. Where two plates carrying continents push against each other, neither can sink easily, because continental rock is relatively light. Instead, the colliding edges crumple and fold upward over millions of years, raising long chains of mountains. The Himalayas, for example, are still rising today as the plate carrying India presses slowly into Asia. Mountains built this way do not last forever. As soon as they rise, wind, water, and ice begin to wear them down, carrying rock away grain by grain. The height of any mountain range therefore reflects a balance between two opposing forces: the slow uplift driven by moving plates and the steady erosion that tears the peaks back down.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: The Himalayas are the tallest mountains on Earth
Moving plates raise mountains, while erosion wears them down
The Earth's shell is a single solid layer
Continental rock is heavier than ocean rock

questions.prompt: The passage compares the movement of tectonic plates to
options: the flow of a fast river
the growth of fingernails
the spin of the Earth
the fall of rain

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that colliding continents form mountains rather than one plate sinking because
options: continental rock is too light to sink easily
the plates stop moving when they meet
erosion pushes the rock upward
the molten rock cools too quickly

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the height of a mountain range reflects
options: the age of the nearest ocean
a balance between uplift and erosion
the number of plates beneath it
how much rain falls each year

sets.explain_zh: 地质学说明文。主旨/细节(指甲生长类比)/推断(轻岩不沉)/细节(隆升与侵蚀平衡)。

sets.passage: For most of human history, people fed themselves by gathering wild plants and hunting animals, moving often to follow their food. Then, beginning roughly ten thousand years ago, groups in several parts of the world independently began to grow crops and raise animals. This shift to farming, sometimes called the Neolithic revolution, transformed human life. Because farmers could store grain, they were able to settle in one place, and permanent villages appeared. A field could feed far more people than the same area of wild land, so populations grew. Settled life also allowed families to own more than they could carry, and differences in wealth became more visible. Larger, stable communities eventually supported people who did not farm at all, such as potters, builders, and priests. Yet the change brought costs as well. Early farmers often worked longer hours than hunters and ate a less varied diet, and crowded villages spread disease more easily. The move to agriculture, then, was less a single triumph than a trade-off whose effects still shape society.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Farming appeared first in a single region
The shift to farming transformed human life but brought both benefits and costs
Hunters always lived longer than farmers
Villages were invented before agriculture

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the ability to store grain allowed people to
options: travel much farther than before
settle in one place and form villages
give up farming entirely
avoid all disease

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that potters and priests could exist because farming
options: produced a surplus that could feed people who did not farm
required no hard physical labor
was practiced only by the wealthy
left people with little to eat

questions.prompt: The author ends by calling agriculture a trade-off mainly in order to
options: prove that farming was a complete mistake
show that the change had drawbacks as well as benefits
argue that hunting should return
explain how crops are grown

sets.explain_zh: 人类学说明文。主旨/细节(存粮定居)/推断(余粮养非农者)/组织(结尾trade-off的用意)。
